question: How does round-robin load balancing differ from least-connections?
answer: Round-robin cycles through servers equally regardless of load; least-connections sends each request to the server with the fewest active connections.
explanation: Round-robin is simple and works when all servers have similar capacity and uniform request processing times. Least-connections adapts to varying load, performing better in heterogeneous environments or when requests differ in complexity.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Client Request] --> B[Load Balancer]
  B --> C{Algorithm}
  C --> D[Round Robin: next server in turn]
  C --> E[Least Connections: server with fewest connections]
  D --> F[Forward to Server A/B/C in order]
  E --> G[Pick Server with lowest load]
  F --> H[Process Request]
  G --> H
  H --> I[Return Response]

question: Design a distributed rate limiter for a global API?
answer: Use a sliding window counter stored in Redis with consistent hashing across shards to enforce limits globally.
explanation: Sliding window counters allow accurate per-user tracking with minimal memory. Consistent hashing distributes keys evenly across Redis nodes, ensuring horizontal scalability. This approach is used by Cloudflare and Databricks for global rate limiting.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Client] --> B[Edge Server]
  B --> C{Check Redis Counter}
  C -->|Under Limit| D[Forward Request]
  C -->|Over Limit| E[Reject with 429]
  D --> F[Increment Counter]

question: What trade-off must be made between consistency and availability in a distributed system?
answer: During a network partition, you must choose between consistency (all nodes see same data) and availability (every request gets a response).
explanation: This is the CAP theorem: a distributed system can only guarantee two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. Since partitions are inevitable, you must choose between CP (consistent but unavailable during splits) and AP (available but possibly stale).
